title: Cat Coughing Up White Foam: Causes and Veterinary Triage Guide
meta_desc: Find out why your cat is coughing up white foam, the urgency level of this symptom, and how to triage the situation before visiting a veterinarian.
content_html:
What is White Foamy Discharge?

Seeing your cat cough up white foam can be alarming, but it is a relatively common symptom. This foam is typically a mixture of saliva, mucus, and air. In many cases, it occurs when a cat has an empty stomach or is experiencing irritation in the esophagus or upper respiratory tract. It can be triggered by something as simple as a hairball or as complex as feline asthma or digestive upset.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a cat coughing up white foam is Medium. If your cat is still active, eating, and breathing comfortably, you should monitor them and schedule a non-emergency appointment. However, if the coughing is persistent, or if your cat appears lethargic, has pale gums, or shows signs of respiratory distress (panting or open-mouth breathing), seek veterinary care immediately.

Why Taking a Photo Helps Triage

It can be difficult for pet owners to distinguish between a true cough, a gag, and vomiting. By taking a clear photo of the foam or, ideally, a video of the episode itself, you provide your veterinarian with vital diagnostic clues. Seeing the consistency of the foam and the physical posture of your cat during the episode helps the vet determine if the issue is coming from the lungs or the stomach.

faqs.question: Can a hairball cause my cat to cough up foam?
faqs.answer: Yes, cats often produce white foam when they are trying to dislodge a hairball that hasn't quite reached the mouth yet.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about white foam?
faqs.answer: You should be concerned if the behavior happens more than once in 24 hours, or if it is accompanied by a lack of appetite and hiding.

faqs.question: Is foamy vomit different from foamy coughing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Coughing is usually a respiratory action, while vomiting involves abdominal contractions. A video helps your vet tell the difference.
